title: PN 226/25: REVISED SYSTEM FOR REQUESTING AN ASSESSMENT IN DEFAULT Background
text:
INCOME TAX DIVISION
The Treasury
Government Office, Douglas
Isle of Man, British Isles
IM1 3TX
Yn Tashtey
Telephone: (01624) 685400
Assessor of Income Tax Email: incometax@itd.treasury.gov.im
Website: www.gov.im/incometax
PRACTICE NOTE
PN 226/25 Date: 31 July 2025
REVISED SYSTEM FOR REQUESTING AN ASSESSMENT IN DEFAULT
Background
Every individual liable to pay income tax is legally obliged to deliver to the Assessor, before 6 October in
every year, a true and correct return of their whole income for the preceding tax year ended on 5 April.
Failure to submit a return by the due date renders the individual liable to an initial penalty, provided by
section 111C (1) of the Income Tax Act 1970.
However, the Assessor recognises that in certain circumstances it may not be possible to submit a fully
completed return by the due date. It has been the practice of the Assessor since 2005 to deal
sympathetically with such circumstances by considering applications from individuals or their appointed
tax agents that an “assessment in default” should be issued and that the initial penalty should not be
imposed.
Section 111F allows the Assessor discretion to not issue a penalty where she is satisfied there is
reasonable excuse for failing to deliver the return. Guidance on this approach was provided in Practice
Notes 113/05 and 177/12.
In recognition of complexity in the tax affairs of some Isle of Man individuals, the Assessor is issuing this
updated guidance which replaces both PN 113/05 and 177/12.
Requesting an Assessment in Default
The Assessor will continue to consider requests from individuals or their appointed tax agent for an
“assessment in default” to be issued and that the initial penalty should not be imposed, where it can be
demonstrated that the return cannot be submitted by the due date, but only where an application is made
within the specified time and the conditions set out below are met.
The Assessor considers that individuals with uncomplicated tax affairs have sufficient time to gather the
information necessary to complete and deliver their tax returns by the due date.
Application for Assessment in Default
For tax return forms for periods ending on or after the 5 April 2025, all applications must be made in
writing on or before 15 September following the year of assessment to which the return relates. The
1

application must include the following information:
 name of the taxpayer
 tax reference number
 tax year to which the return relates; and
 which of the conditions detailed below apply
Any application received which does not include the above information or is received after the deadline will
be rejected.
The Assessor will accept an email request from an individual as long as the email address is held by the
Assessor on file as the email address which has already been authorised by the taxpayer for
correspondence. The Assessor will accept an email request from an Agent as long as the email is from the
Agent’s work email address (or where acting as personal Agent, the email held with Income Tax Division)
and the Agent authority for the taxpayer is already in place with the Income Tax Division. Please include
“Assessment in Default Request” as the subject of any email request made.
There is no set format for an application, but it may be beneficial for applicants or tax agents to set out
applications in the format below:
Name of Taxpayer Tax Reference Number Tax Year Condition for Application
Conditions
The individual must meet one or more of the following conditions in order for an assessment in default
request to be considered:
 Have income from Self-employment;
 Have Rental Income from properties in the Isle of Man;
 Have Overseas Rental Income or other Overseas income that has been taxed;
 Be a Mariner;
 Be a Shareholder in an Isle of Man company and have received distributions from the company in
the period;
 Have other complex tax affairs which may delay the establishment of final income figures; or
 Have experienced exceptional personal circumstances immediately before the due date, such as
serious illness or unplanned temporary absence from the Island.
The application can be in respect of one tax return only, it cannot apply retrospectively or be for future tax
returns.
Even if one of the above conditions are met, the Assessor will refuse any applications where the following
circumstances apply to the individual:
 Outstanding tax return forms for earlier years;
 Outstanding tax, National Insurance and/or related interest or penalty debt unless an up to date
payment arrangement is in force;
 Provisional figures provided with an earlier tax year’s return form have not been replaced with the
definitive figures;
 Previous tax years’ return forms that had an approved default request were not received by the
following 6 April; or
 The individual has a general poor compliance history.
2

The text above refers to individuals, but the application will be extended to partnerships and Limited Liability
Companies (LLCs) where all the partners or members are individuals. However, where a partnership or LLC
is required to demonstrate adequate substance in the Isle of Man, then the partnership/LLC will not be
permitted to request an assessment in default and the return form must be submitted by the statutory due
date, in order to avoid the issue of any late return form penalty.
This Division will inform the individual or their appointed Agent when an application for an assessment in
default is rejected.
If the application is accepted, an assessment in default will be issued. The Division will use its normal
procedures in arriving at the amounts to be included in the default assessment.
Please note that there is no right to appeal against an assessment in default.
Return Submission
If the return which has been the subject of an application for an assessment in default, has not been
submitted to the Income Tax Division within 12 months of the end of the year of assessment to which it
relates (i.e. the following 5 April), then both the extended penalty provided by section 111D of the Income
Tax Act 1970 which becomes chargeable on that date, and the initial penalty provided for under section
111C, will be imposed.
The Assessor encourages all individuals to submit their returns using Online Tax Services, as some
information in the return is pre-populated based on information received from third parties, such as
employers. Registration for Online Tax Services can be made through Isle of Man Government’s online
services website. As the process for online registration requires the issue of an authorisation code by
mail, it is recommended to register as early as possible before any deadlines.
Payment and Postponement of Liabilities
The due date for payment of tax when a default assessment has been issued is fixed at the due date for
that default assessment, not the date of the definitive assessment issued later, (i.e. by the later of 6
January following the year of assessment or 30 days from the date of issue of the default assessment).
If, after having received an assessment in default, the individual or their appointed tax Agent believes that
the tax liability in the default assessment shown is lower than the amount believed to be due for the year,
it is recommended to pay the higher amount. This is in order to avoid being charged late payment
interest on any additional amount when the final definitive assessment is issued.
If you believe that the tax liability shown in the default is higher than the amount you have calculated, the
lower amount can be paid.
However, as there is no provision in the legislation to allow a postponement request in relation to a
default assessment tax liability, a partial payment will not stop the issue of reminders for the balance of a
default assessment tax liability. Reminders for outstanding amounts relating to default assessments will
cease once the tax return has been submitted and a final assessment has been issued.
Repayment Supplement
Section 107B (10) of the Income Tax Act 1970 provides that there will be no repayment supplement in
respect of any repayment of tax where an assessment in default has been issued in respect of the year of
assessment to which the return relates, or a section 111D penalty for extended default has been made for
the year of assessment.
3

As an assessment in default will be issued when a request under this guidance is accepted, no repayment
supplement will be payable where that assessment is subsequently revised and a refund becomes due.
Any delay in the submission of a tax return form should be rectified at the earliest possible opportunity.
Assessor of Income Tax
This Practice Note is intended only as a general guide and must be read in conjunction with the appropriate
legislation. It does not have any binding force and does not affect a person’s right of appeal on points concerning
their own liability to income tax.
Comments and suggestions for improvements of issued Practice Notes and suggestions for future Practice Notes
are always welcome.
4
